title: iPhone 17 Pro Colour Change 🤯 New Problem 🔥 #shorts #smartphone #iphone
transcript: Brother, it has been barely 1 1/2 months since the launch of the iPhone 17 series and now a new problem has come up inside it.  Well, you all must remember that when the iPhone 17 series was launched, after that there were a lot of complaints that it was getting too many scratches, too many dents and it was getting bent very easily. But brother, now a very strange problem is coming in the iPhone 17 Pro series. Brother, many users are complaining that their Cosmic Orange color iPhone 17 Pro is gradually changing to pink color.  The reason behind this is being told that this time Apple used anodized aluminum instead of titanium, which reacts in certain conditions and its color starts changing.  This problem is mostly occurring with the Orange colour Pro series users.  Now here some iPhone lovers are saying that this is a hidden feature of Apple. title: iPhone 17 Pro Max 1 Month Later There Are Some Issues !!
transcript: So, I've been using the iPhone 17 Pro Max for a while now, and being honest, this iPhone is great, but it has a few small issues. First up, I went with the silver color. I know a lot of people are into that shiny, vibrant orange color, but it wasn't for me. I went for a much more muted and timeless color. It looks great even with a case, and the most important part is that it is a lot less prone to scratches and scuffs. This year, Apple used aluminum instead of titanium on their Pro models. Aluminum definitely helps with heat dissipation, but at the end of the day, it scratches a lot easier, and if you don't use a case, it can be a nightmare to keep this phone in pristine condition. I'm using this clear case for my iPhone 17 Pro Max, and it does the job pretty well. I have a few other cases, too, from Pitaka. They go really well with my silver iPhone 17 Pro Max. Orange iPhones are already turning pink, so there's definitely some sort of durability issue with this year's iPhone model. Anyway, the rest of the phone has been great. The only issue I faced was random Wi-Fi disconnection. I'm using the 512 GB model, but it randomly disconnects from my home Wi-Fi network. This issue isn't there on any of my other Apple devices. I checked out the internet and it seems the issue is widely spread. I updated to iOS 26.0.1, but the issue was still there. And I even upgraded to iOS 26.1 beta 4 and this random disconnection issue is still there. I don't know if this is a hardware issue or a software glitch that is affecting my unit. Apart from that, I'm facing no issues at all. The performance has been great and the cameras are superb. The telephoto camera, to be precise, is also doing great. There's not a massive difference in quality and performance when comparing it with the iPhone 16 Pro Max, but now the image is cleaner and noise-free at higher zoom levels. The PWM dimming technology is helping me, too. I've been getting less eye strain lately after turning on this feature. If you have an iPhone 17 model, I'd highly recommend turning on this option if you get a lot of eye strain. I'm using a screen protector, so I wasn't able to test the new anti-reflective coating. Some people on the internet are saying not to use a screen protector at all, as it can ruin the anti-reflective coating. But for me, the safety of the display comes first, which is why I'm using this borderless screen protector. The display this year is a lot brighter and now it goes up to 3000 nits. Although I use my iPhone mostly indoors, so I'm not really benefiting from that. Sound quality is about the same as the iPhone 16 Pro. I haven't noticed any drastic difference in sound quality. Moving on to battery life, the most crucial part for a lot of people. Battery life on this iPhone is just crazy good. Many tests like from Phone Buff already show the winner is clearly the iPhone 17 Pro Max, beating the Galaxy S25 Ultra from Samsung. It's easily a 2-day battery life smartphone for me. I charge it daily and I have kept the battery charging limit to 80% as it helps preserve the battery health. Overall, the iPhone 17 Pro and Pro Max are solid phones from Apple this year. There are a few issues like the scratch gate and Wi-Fi issues, but other than that, this phone is phenomenal. If you have an iPhone 15 Pro or older, I'd suggest trying out this phone for once, and you'll definitely like it a lot. If you're using an iPhone 17 or 17 Pro, then let me know in the comments how your experience has been so far. And with that said, I'll catch you in the next one.

title: iPhone 17 Pro Max vs OPPO X9 Pro — Which Camera Wins the Battle?
transcript: This here is the Oppo Find X9 Pro, and it might be one of the most over-the-top camera phones I've ever used. We're talking about a 200 megapixel, three times telephoto, hassleblack color tuning, log video for creators, and a 7500 millia battery that just refuses to die. Now, I did partner with Oppo on this video, but all the thoughts and testing are mine. Also, quick thing before we continue, about 80% of you watching my videos haven't subscribed, and honestly, I find that incredible. If you're already here and you clearly like tech, it just takes one click. Plus, it will help you make sure you don't ever miss [music] any of my dad jokes. But let's start with the biggest improvements, and that's hands down the cameras. This is where the Find X9 Pro goes allin. You've got a 50 megapixel main lens, a 50 megapixel ultrawide, and a 200 megapixel three times telephoto. All tuned by Hasselblad. [music] Color consistency across all three lenses is solid. OPPO nails that vibrant, slightly cinematic look, while iPhone sticks to its more natural, contrast, heavy tone. If you like your photos ready to post to social media, OPPO leans towards that look. It's especially apparent with the bluer skies and brighter greens. But daylight shots, both are fantastic. The iPhone tends to go moodier with deeper shadows, and open brightens things up with a bit more punch. The ultrawide is crisp on both though Apple gives you a slightly [music] wider field of view using a.5x field of view compared to 0.6 on the OPPO X9 Pro. Also, you have the option to use the entire 200 megapixel sensor by activating Hasselblad high-res mode, which produces shots up to 16K resolution and some pretty incredible bokeh. Just note that if you do use this mode, it's best to do it with optimal lighting conditions to get the best results. Speaking of results, the same three times mode is used for macro photography, which automatically kicks in when you are 2 to 4 cm close to the subject. But once you start zooming in, especially beyond 10 times, the OPPO starts to pull away. That 200 megapixel sensor actually makes a difference. Details stay sharper, colors stay true, and even at 30 [music] times, images are totally usable. Meanwhile, Apple caps out around 40 times digital, but the processing gets pretty aggressive. And if you're a serious photographer who likes to use their smartphones for photography, OPPO's got this Hasselblad teleconverter, a magnetic lens attachment that gives you about a 3.28 times optical magnification. [music] Now, it's surprisingly good, especially for wildlife, sports, or concerts. I don't usually carry large smartphone lenses, but this one is legit if you're super into smartphone photography. It even feels wellmade like an expensive lens you'd buy for a professional camera. Low light, though, this is where OPPO really surprised me. It consistently delivers brighter, cleaner images with less noise than the iPhone in most situations. Apple still does a better job preserving contrast and highlight control, but OPPO's night mode just pulls in way more light, offering a cleaner yet brighter image to work with. There's even a new stage mode for concerts that focuses on performers and balances exposure under bright spotlights. title: vivo X300 Pro vs. iPhone 17 Pro Max: This is Embarrassing!
transcript: ﻿The Vivo X300 Pro is not only an amazing camera  phone that uses the famous Zeiss coating on its   large camera bump and huge telephoto sensor,  but it also has a big battery on its back. So   naturally, I decided to compare it to the iPhone  17 Pro Max to see if it can take better pictures   and also if the battery life lasts longer. I am  Mo and I bought both phones with my own money.   I’ll start with the camera comparison because I  know most of you are here to see that. Starting   with the main lens, the Vivo and iPhone main  sensors are the same size, but the Vivo has an   f/1.6 aperture instead of f/1.8; therefore, you  get slightly more light. In practical terms, you   can also see that the Vivo has a little bit more  background blur or bokeh because of that. But the   main difference is in color science. I’m taking  photos with Zeiss Natural Mode, and in this mode,   at least, the Vivo has a bit more of a magenta  tint while the iPhone leans toward green. That   makes the skin tone slightly more lively on the  Vivo, and the colors are also a bit more vivid.   Also, if you look at Chilli the dog, his white  is whiter on the Vivo than on the iPhone. For   indoor shots and Tanya’s skin tone, the Vivo is  warmer, and the iPhone makes her colder and more   contrasty. The same goes for my skin tone — I’m  darker on the iPhone and a bit more orange on the   Vivo, and I can tell you the reality is something  in between. For Chilli the dog’s skin tone, I feel   the iPhone is nailing the color better. Yes, the  white balance leans a bit too much into green,   but the exposure and overall picture look nicer  on the iPhone. You can also use portrait mode,   and I honestly prefer the shots on the  Vivo. Yes, the skin is a bit smoother,   but that’s what I personally prefer when I take  portrait shots — I don’t like too much contrast.   You can also use filters on both phones. For  iPhone, the filters are more about undertones and   slight color changes, but for Vivo, you have these  filmic looks that remind me of Fujifilm cameras,   and I really dig it. The magic of the Vivo X300  Pro is more in the telephoto lens, where it uses   a much larger sensor than the iPhone. It’s also  a 200MP one instead of 48MP, and it’s an 85mm   lens instead of 100mm on the iPhone — and 85mm  is my favorite focal length for portrait shots. If you look at this picture, you’ll notice  that the background blur or bokeh on the   Vivo is stronger, making the photo look more  professional. But more importantly, the better   sensor also lets the phone produce better colors.  I can assure you, Maria’s hair is not gray like   in the photo the iPhone took, and her face  is not that pale. For moving pets and kids,   the Vivo is doing a better job too — again, the  larger sensor lets the phone capture moments with   a higher shutter speed, which helps freeze motion  better. If you zoom further with the phones, the   Vivo also seems to use some new GenAI algorithms  because the face looks really smooth, but I’m not   complaining as long as the photo looks better. If you zoom really far, the Vivo is definitely   using GenAI to reconstruct lost information —  something that Pixel phones also do these days.   You can also take macro shots with  the telephoto lens on the Vivo. You   can technically do the same on the iPhone, but  the result is not even close. You can also use   the ultrawide camera for macro shots, and I do  find the Vivo nailing highlights better here. For daylight photos with the ultrawide lens, the  difference is more in color science, where the   Vivo makes the sky more vibrant — again, in Zeiss  Natural Mode. But on the other hand, I find the   Vivo doing better body proportion corrections, as  you can see in this shot — the iPhone photo looks   unnatural. And talking about colors, generally  Vivo’s colors are more vivid even if you don’t   use the vivid mode. But they’re also more accurate  — like in this photo, the iPhone looks so dull and   the white balance is completely wrong, whereas  the Vivo nailed the autumn vibe. Or in this shot,   the iPhone’s colors are just wrong, and it  doesn’t only look bad but also inaccurate.   I think Chinese phones usually get a bad rep  for artificial colors, but in my experience,   at least Vivo is producing better colors than the  iPhone. One thing that the iPhone does better,   though, is selfies for sure — the camera simply  produces better results. Also, the selfie camera   on the iPhone is square, so you can take both  horizontal and vertical photos. For night and   low-light photos, I took this photo in almost  absolute darkness. The Vivo is producing a better   result even with a shutter speed of 1/6s on the  main lens. And of course, with the telephoto lens,   it’s not even a comparison — the Vivo photo is  head and shoulders above the iPhone. For videos,   the game changes though — the Vivo video has  some lags and frame drops for some reason,   even during daylight with the main lens. Both  phones can also shoot at 120FPS, but again,   the iPhone is smoother, and the Vivo video has  some focus problems for some reason, making the   focus plane change often. For portrait video, or  “Cinematic Mode” as iPhone calls it, they both   do a good job. It does seem Vivo is applying some  sort of filter, though, and I think probably girls   like that more — but you can also argue it’s less  realistic. Another thing to note about the camera   is that the Vivo has a rather big camera bump. It  can be used as a resting place for your finger,   but it also sometimes makes handling the phone  slightly inconvenient. If you look at the display,   the Vivo has a punch hole while the iPhone has  a Dynamic Island. The Vivo has slightly thinner   bezels, whereas the iPhone is slightly larger  overall. The screens on both are flat — thank   God. The iPhone has an anti-reflective coating,  which is nice, but on the other hand, the Vivo   comes with a screen protector pre-installed in the  box. The charging speed of the Vivo is 90W max,   while the iPhone is 40W — and also, on the Chinese  variant that I got, there’s a charger in the box!   The version I’m using is running OriginOS,  and they do promise 4 years of updates. BTW,   as a side note, if you want to get the Vivo, I’ll  put the link in the description. I got mine from   TradingShenzhen — I’ve been buying phones from  them for two years, and they have never ever   disappointed me. The OriginOS looks suspiciously  similar to iOS — even the camera icon is kind of   a copy-paste. If you go to the quick settings, it  also has this very similar liquid glass kind of   feel to it. The animations are also suspiciously  similar — I mean, they’re not even trying to hide   it at this point. But of course, at the end of the  day, OriginOS is Android at its core, whereas the   iPhone is running iOS, and they are fundamentally  different except for the look and feel. Also,   if you do consider buying the Chinese variant of  the Vivo, just know that it may not run Android   Auto for your car, and you’ll also see some random  Chinese characters here and there — which you can,   of course, change and delete, but you’ll never  fully get rid of them. On the other hand, on the   Chinese variant of the Vivo, you get a 6500 mAh  battery, while on the global one, it’s only 5500   mAh — which makes a huge difference. Talking about  huge and big things — thanks for 67K subscribers,   already a great number ! I’m trying to get to 69K,  so please give a hand and subscribe to the channel   if you haven’t already.I did a battery test on  the phones with lots of video and photos, cycling,   GPS tracking, YouTube, Google Meet calls, on a  mix of Wi-Fi and 5G — and by the end of the test,   the Vivo had 46% battery left, and the  iPhone had 31%, which is insane because   the iPhone is already the best battery  life phone among conventional flagships.   The other thing you may be curious about is  how the new Dimensity 9500 processor on the   Vivo X300 Pro stacks up against the iPhone A19  Pro and other flagship phones. I actually made   a full video about this, which you can watch  next — but spoiler alert, the processor is as   good as the one on the iPhone. Thanks for tuning  into Techmo, and I’ll see you in the next one!

title: iPhone 17 Pro Max vs Galaxy S25 Ultra – LOG Video Test
transcript: Smartphones are becoming very capable devices when it comes to shooting video. Now, if you've ever done any kind of professional video shooting, you've most likely shot in a log format. But so recently, smartphones [music] have been including the capabilities of capturing log footage. Both the Samsung S25 Ultra and the iPhone 17 Pro Max capture log footage. Samsung log on the S25 Ultra and then Apple log on the iPhone. I will say Apple has really pushed the boundaries in terms of what you can capture with your phone with their ProRes mode. But so I wanted to do just kind kind of a very casual test between these two devices. I'm short on time this week and actually this next month I will be very busy. I'm moving, doing all sorts of things. So, a lot of my videos will probably have to be a little bit on the shorter side, but in this video, I captured very similar clips with the iPhone 17 Pro Max and the Galaxy S25 Ultra. title: OnePlus 15 vs iPhone 17 Pro Max - The Real Flagship!
transcript: So, the stage is set for one of the biggest flagship battles of the year, the OnePlus 15 versus the iPhone 17 Pro Max. Both phones are pushing smartphone technology to its limits, but they do it in very different ways. OnePlus focuses on raw performance and futuristic hardware, while Apple refineses everything to perfection with power, precision, and ecosystem [music] integration. Let's see how these two titans compare across display, performance, cameras, and overall experience. Starting with the display, OnePlus seems ready to dominate the specs race. The OnePlus 15 will feature a 6.78 in LTPO OLED panel with a blazing fast 165 Hz refresh rate, the highest ever on a mainstream flagship. Paired with a 1.5K resolution, it promises smoother visuals and better battery efficiency than traditional 2K screens. Meanwhile, the iPhone 17 Pro Max sticks to Apple's proven 6.9 in super retina XDR display with prootion 120 Hz. It might not sound as flashy on paper, but its color calibration, brightness, and HDR performance make it one of the best panels for media consumption and daily use. Moving to performance, both phones bring nextG processors. The OnePlus 15 is powered by Qualcomm's Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 paired with an upgraded cooling system for sustained gaming performance. Powering it all is a massive 7,300 mAh Glacier battery capable of 120 watts wired and 50 Ws wireless charging. An absolute speed demon when it comes to refueling. The iPhone 17 Pro Max, on the other hand, runs Apple's brand new A19 Pro chip built on a 3 nanometer process. Apple's silicon is still the king of efficiency and realworld performance, delivering smoother multitasking, better thermal control, and superior AI integration. Though, you'll need to live with slower charging speeds compared to OnePlus. Now, let's talk about cameras, where things get [music] intense. The OnePlus 15 packs a triple 50 megapixel camera, including wide, ultra wide, and a telephoto lens, offering up to 3.5 times optical zoom. With new image processing improvements, OnePlus aims to close the gap with Apple in low light and portrait shots. But the iPhone 17 Pro Max still sets the standard with its 48 megapixel triple camera array and a game-changing 8x optical quality zoom lens. Combine that with Apple's unmatched video performance and color accuracy, and the iPhone remains a content creator's [music] favorite. In terms of software, the story remains familiar. Oxygen OS 16 versus iOS 19. OnePlus offers customization, speed, and a near stock Android experience. While Apple delivers unmatched ecosystem harmony, years of updates, and flawless optimization. If freedom and flexibility matter most, OnePlus wins. But if stability, reliability, and seamless device integration are your priority, Apple still holds the crown. [music] In conclusion, the OnePlus 15 is the ultimate performance powerhouse built for speed gaming and fast charging. The iPhone 17 Pro Max, however, is the definition of refinement, delivering elite performance, incredible cameras, and unmatched ecosystem polish. So, what do you guys think about both the devices? Let me know your thoughts in the comments down below. And as always, I will see you guys in my next

title: OPPO Find X9 Pro vs. iPhone 17 Pro Max vs. Galaxy S25 Ultra Battery Test
transcript: The iPhone 17 Pro Max recently became the longest lasting iPhone we've ever tested by a big margin. But today, it's going up against what might just be the longest lasting phone, period, in the all-new Oppo Find X9 Pro, which is not only powered by MediaTek's new 3nm Demenity 9500 chip, but it features the biggest battery we have ever tested in a phone at a massive 7,500 MIA. That's roughly 50% bigger than the battery on both the iPhone and the Galaxy S25 Ultra, which gives the OPPO a huge advantage. Now, obviously, the iPhone isn't a slouch. It's powered by Apple's A19 Pro chip. And it benefits from the tight hardware and software integration that Apple is known for. Whether or not that'll be enough for the iPhone to hold its ground, though, well, there's only one way to find out. But before we do, quick disclosure. We partnered with OPPO on the channel, and they did give us this unit for testing. But APO didn't have a say in our methodology, nor did they get to see this video before publishing. So with that out of the way, let's get right into it. All right, we'll kick things off in the phone test where after going on the same 1-hour phone call, the FindX 9 Pro is still reporting 100%. The iPhone is at 99% while the Galaxy drops down to 96. Now, obviously, there's no way that the OPPO didn't use at least some of its battery in that phone test. So, I think we'll get a much better idea of how it's actually doing here in messaging where we have each phone texting back and forth with our automated chatbot where an hour of texting later, this time we do see some drain on the Oppo, although it's only by four points down to 96. While the iPhone and the Galaxy aren't that far behind, each dropping by five points. Here in email, we're not doing anything too intensive. We're just opening up the same set of emails and then scrolling through them. Where, as always, we've calibrated each display to the same 200 nits of brightness. And 60 minutes into it, all three phones do really well. The iPhone and the Oppo actually tie there, which is just impressive on the iPhone's part, given how much smaller its battery is, while the Galaxy only does one point worse. meaning heading into browser, this is still technically anyone's game. Now, this browser test though is definitely more intensive than email was since we're randomly cycling through the same set of websites and we're scrolling through them where we'll actually get to see how each chipset performs where we are talking about three completely different SOC's with MediaTek's Demenity 9500 in Yappo, Apple's own A19 Pro in the iPhone, and Qualcomm's Snapdragon 8 Elite chip in the Galaxy where after an hour the FX9 Pro does the best with only a six points drop. While the iPhone actually takes the biggest hit with a 9point drop. But if we were talking strictly in terms of efficiency and the estimated milliamp usage, the Galaxy actually did the best in that test with it technically using the least amount of its battery capacity. Here in Instagram, we're just scrolling through the home feed like you would in real life, where this test probably stresses the dynamic refresh rates on the phones more so than anything else, where all three phones do roughly the same in terms of the percentage points drops with the iPhone this time being the most efficient in terms of its estimated millia usage. The Apple was technically the least efficient in that test, but of course, because of its 7,500 mAh capacity, it was still able to match the iPhone in terms of the absolute percentage point drop. And because of that capacity, it's likely going to have a major advantage here in standby where typically the phones with bigger capacities tend to do better. Where after 16 hours of just sitting there on idle, that's exactly what we see. There's only a four point drop on a Diapo, a fivepoint drop on the Galaxy, which is actually really good given its battery size, and then a 10point drop on a iPhone. So definitely a big hit to the iPhone's battery. as we move on to YouTube. So, it's worth noting here that in addition to having the screens calibrated, we also calibrate the speakers on each phone to the nearest decel count. That way, we're actually testing the phones and not just some random variables, where after an hour of binge watching some phone buff, the FX9 Pro does the best again, dropping by only six points, while the iPhone drops by seven and the Galaxy by eight. In this gaming test, we have each phone playing Alto's Adventure by continuously tapping on the screen to make sure that we're keeping the touchcreens active, where this time we see a much bigger difference. The FX9 Pro drains by only five points there, compared to a 9-point drop on the iPhone and an 8-point drop on the Galaxy, allowing the Oppo to pull a full 15 points ahead of the other two. But look, we're still relatively early in this test. Each phone still has over 50% battery remaining, and the iPhone specifically has this knack for making comebacks as the test progresses. But at least after an hour of simulated navigation, the trend only continues with the Oppo doing one point better with a fivepoint drain compared to the sixpoint drain on both the iPhone and Galaxy. Here in Spotify, our robots are just listening to music, which, you know, at first glance doesn't seem like the most intensive task out there. But because we have each phone continuously playing back music with their speakers calibrated, it does tend to tax the batteries quite a bit. And one thing worth remembering is this is one of those tasks where software optimization can't really do much to help. Meaning, outside of the speakers themselves, the battery capacity becomes the biggest factor. where 60 minutes later, wow, we only see a fivepoint drop on the BX9 Pro compared to the 8point drain on the iPhone and a 9-point drop on the S25 Ultra. So, for the Oppo, I think that's the least I've seen a phone drain in Spotify given how intensive it is, which makes me super curious as to how it'll do here in Snapchat since this one is the most intensive in the entire lineup with us not only taxing the screen and the camera as we record these clips, but we're also using the microphone, we're using the speakers, and the phones are even using Wi-Fi as we upload each clip to Snap's servers where after an hour of doing this in a loop, oh man, only a 7oint drain on the Oppo compared to the 13 and 17 point drain on the iPhone and Galaxy. So, not just better in terms of the absolute drain for the Oppo there, but also in terms of efficiency, which means for the first time ever, we have a phone going into app cycle with 50% still remaining. I mean, a few years ago, no phone made it this far. So, seeing one doing this well at this stage is insane. Now, this app cycle test is also super intensive with us opening and closing the same set of apps in a loop where after an hour and 50 minutes of doing so, the Galaxy finally gives out. I mean, that's a respectable performance, but the iPhone is still at 9% at this point, while the OPPO is at a whopping 33%. So, just a huge gap in terms of the performance between the Android phones. But I want to see what the difference is going to be with the iPhone where even though the iPhone 17 Pro Max broke Apple's records lasting longer than any iPhone that ever came before it at a full 3 hours and 5 minutes into app cycle, it's still short of what OPPO is doing with it still at a healthy 21%. So there you have it. I mean, that is a huge delta, which just to put it into perspective outside of this intensive app cycle, that 21% is good enough to last you 3 hours and 30 minutes of web browsing or up to 5 hours and 15 minutes of messaging. But at least in Appcycle, it's enough to get the Find X9 Pro the top spot in our rankings, officially beating the Vivo X200 Pro with 13% still left to go, allowing it to not only pass the 4hour mark in app cycle, but officially going for 5 hours and 12 minutes before getting down to its last percent, at which point it enters this ultra low power saving mode that we couldn't disable. So technically at this point the app cycle portion of this test is done since we can't open and close the apps like we normally do. So as a partial solution we had the phone go through the browser test again since you know that was pretty intensive. And we did this for a full 30 minutes and then we switched over to Snap where the FX9 Pro then finally gave out. So you know at least officially its total time is 31 hours and 12 minutes into this test. But if we also count the ultra low power mode, it adds over 30 minutes of extra time, making the winner and the new foam buff style battery test champ by a big margin, the Finded X9 Pro. Anyways, that is it for me in this video. Thank you for watching and as always, I'll see you in the very next episode.

On the iPhone, you can only control your frame rate and also the exposure and brightness of the image. Beyond that, you can't do anything. So, that's the first point to consider regarding Samsung. Now let's look at the difference between them in the image itself. Of course, the images you see here have been edited and the colors adjusted according to the chart I have in front of me. First, the video file on the Samsung was slightly less than 900 MB, while on the iPhone it was more than 8 GB. So, of course, there's a huge difference in size—eight times the size. Second, note that the lighting on both phones is different and changes; it gets darker and brighter. I kept everything still, and nothing changed except for me, for example, moving a little left and right. Now, on the iPhone 17 Pro and Pro Max, if you're using another app like Blackmagic Camera or Final Cut Camera, you can shoot Pro Res Log 2 or Pro Res Ro and also use the Open Gear feature. The first thing I noticed is that the frame angle is much wider than on the Samsung. I also noticed that the dynamic range on the iPhone is better than on the Samsung, which is evident in the harsh light on my back and the shadows on my face. The details are much better on the iPhone, and there's less noise. Another advantage of this mode is that the lighting on the iPhone is...  It didn't change at all; there was no change in the brightness of the lighting. The strange thing is that the controls on the Final Cut Camera are the same as those on the Pro on Samsung, even though the Pro Video on Samsung has more features, like showing false cherries and zippers. But on the Final Cut Camera, the lighting remained constant, as we mentioned. Another thing that distinguishes the shooting on the Final Cut Camera is that you can shoot at the same frame rate as your camera, not just 30 frames per second, but also 29.97. This is much better if you're using two cameras simultaneously—your camera and your phone—because when there's a cut, there's no difference in the audio. This used to happen to me a lot when I was shooting at 30 frames per second. What also surprised me was that the video file size of the Pro Rise Log on the Final Cut Camera was smaller than the Pro Rise Log on the Samsung.  This is a screenshot from the camera app, as its size here was approximately 6 GB, while as we mentioned in the second video, it was over 8 GB. The difference between the two was less than 10 seconds. Now, let's talk about the big beast, the Pro Rise Ro, with its OpenGate shooting. OpenGate means it shoots with the entire sensor, not just a part of it like all its other phones. This is evident in the frame rate difference between the two; there's a huge difference. It feels like I'm shooting with the ultrawide camera, even though I'm shooting with the regular wide-angle camera. Plus, you have the flexibility to adjust the colors, dynamic range, and white balance as you like. But here, to make things a bit more balanced, I simply corrected the colors, and the image quality is truly exceptional—much higher than Samsung's and even higher than the Pro Rise Log's recording. Keep in mind that I'm not the best at color correction for videos, nor am I the one who adds cinematic color effects.  I think that someone more professional than me, or someone who knows how to adjust colors better, would get a much better result with a feature like this than what I achieved. Also, the video file size is 12 GB and a bit, much larger than the previous ones, even though the video itself is about 15 seconds longer. But now for the most important question: which phone can you use for professional photography, replacing your camera or acting as a sleeve for your camera? I think both will give you excellent performance. Perhaps the only drawback of the Samsung phone that I can mention is that its lighting is inconsistent. I think if you use an app like Black Magic Camera available on Android, this issue will be resolved and won't be a problem. Unfortunately, I couldn't use that app, and in fact, the first test I did was filmed entirely with it, but all the colors were messed up. I couldn't adjust the colors and settings on the phone to be able to edit them better on my computer, and this is one of the reasons...  The reasons I used the Final Cut camera after running the test on both phones are that I was truly impressed by the Samsung's performance. I didn't expect it to produce such good results with such vibrant colors and overall quality. As for the iPhone, I think it offers more flexibility. There are different shooting modes you can use. You can shoot Pro Res, Lock Tome, with better colors and a better dynamic range. You can also shoot Pro Res Raw if you're a professional and can utilize those capabilities. Furthermore, if you're using professional-level features like Time Code, you can also use that feature on the iPhone 17 Pro Max. This is what gives the iPhone higher scores in professional photography. If you'd like to see a photography comparison between the phones and the camera I use under the same shooting conditions and setup, watch these videos and then write your opinion below. Don't forget to tell me in the comments which phone you preferred for professional photography. Don't forget to like, share, and follow me. See you in the next video, God willing. Peace.

title: iPhone 17 & 17 Pro (Max) Camera & Photo Settings Tutorial
transcript: There are many useful features hidden behind the  simple interface of the iPhone Camera app. Today,   I'll show you everything you need to know to get  the best possible results with your iPhone 17,   17 Air, and of course the 17 Pro and Pro  Max. I Will focus on the photo settings.   For the video settings there will be  another video. For a better overview,   the video is divided into 20 parts corresponding  to the most important camera features. The chapter   titles allow you to quickly find specific parts. And there's something else I want to show you   today. You'll see that for the Night Mode  or even when using the new selfie camera,   it can be extremely useful to be able to place  your iPhone in a stable position. That's why   I looked for a simple solution to quickly and  securely place my iPhone in an upright position.   And I found a great solution for the iPhone  17 Pro – which I'll show you a little later. You can open the camera app in different ways:  Of course by tapping the app icon, in the control   center you will find it in the lower right corner,  from the lock screen by swiping to the left or by   pressing the camera icon, and of course by  pressing the camera control button. But this   button can do much more than just open the camera  - I'll show you all its features a little later. Let's start simple, with the  basic operation of the app.  When you start the camera app, you  will see the shutter button here,   a small preview of your last shot at  the bottom left, and the small icon   on the right allows you to switch to the  front camera, which has received a number   of new features for the iPhone 17, which  we will also take a closer look at later. Here in the middle, you can switch  between shooting modes. Initially,   you will only see the photo and video  modes, but if you swipe left or right,   the other shooting modes will also appear. On the right there are the four photo modes Photo,   Portrait, Spatial and Pano. on the left you  find the four video modes Video, Cinematic,   Slo-Mo and Time-Lapse. While photo and video are  self-explanatory, you can use Portrait to create   a blurred background. Although this actually also  works with objects, the mode is called Portrait,   because this effect is especially well suited  for portraits. With Spatial you can not only   take photos but also videos with a spatial 3D  effect and then play them back on the Vision Pro   or the Meta Quest headsets. If you do not have  a VR Headset, this mode is currently useless.  In Panorama mode you can capture images  with a field of view that is much wider   than the camera lens would actually allow. This  is especially suitable for landscape photography,   for example. The Cinematic Mode is a kind of  Portrait Mode for video. And with the slow-motion   mode and the timelapse mode, you can take cool  slow-motion, time-lapse, or hyperlapse shots.  But as I said, today we are  talking about the photo modes. Let's first have a look at the different  functions of the shutter button. If you   want to take a simple photo, of course  you tap the shutter button briefly. You   can achieve the same result by using the  volume buttons. It makes no difference   which of the two buttons you use. If you want  to quickly capture several photos in a row,   you can swipe the shutter button to the left. Now  your Iphone will capture photos in an extremely   fast sequence until you lift your finger. This  is called the Burst Mode. This feature is often   used for photographing action. But it can also  help when taking portraits, for example to avoid   closed eyes. You simply take several photos in  quick succession and then select the best one.  By the way, in the settings you can also set the  Burst Mode to be activated by using the Volume Up   button. This would be especially interesting  if you want to use this mode frequently. If you press and hold the Shutter button,  the Iphone automatically starts to capture   a video. The recording ends as soon as you  release the Shutter button. This way you   can quickly capture a video without having  to switch to video mode first. Apple calls   this QuickTake. If you want to capture a longer  video then drag the shutter right onto the lock.   Now the Iphone will continue recording even if  you release the shutter button. While recording   you can also continue to take pictures by  tapping on the shutter button that appears   on the right. And here on the left you can  pause and resume the recording. You can also   start capturing a QuickTake video by pressing  and holding one of the two volume buttons. Now let's take a look at the zoom and the lenses. Above the menu bar you can see a small 1x. It   is yellow. This means that the main camera is  currently active. By tapping on the 0.5 to the   left of it, you activate the ultra-wide camera  and via the 4 the camera with the telephoto   lens. However, the latter is only available  on the two Pro models. The 2 and the 8 do not   stand for additional cameras. Rather, only a  smaller part of the sensor is used for taking   the picture. Nevertheless, the high-resolution  48-megapixel sensors enable high-quality results.  And the high-resolution sensor of the main  camera also makes another feature possible:   if you tap on the 1, you can activate  a focal length of 28mm, that is 1.2x,   and 35mm, that is 1.5x. Yes, this is also a  digital crop, but similar to the case of the   2x zoom, there is hardly any loss of quality  compared to the native focal length of 1x,   certainly also because the images  are digitally processed again.  The default field of view of the main camera is  relatively wide at 24mm, or 1x. If you prefer that   the main camera is set to 1.2x or 1.5x by default  when you start the camera app, you can set that in   the settings of the camera app under FusionCamera  and Default Lens. This is a useful option because,   for example, 35mm, which is 1.5x, is considered  the most flexible focal length to use.  When choosing a lens, one thing should  be clear to you. Even if it does not   look like this on the display, the different  lenses have different technical properties.  The main camera produces less distortions  than the ultra-wide camera and it has the   largest aperture. It can therefore achieve better  results in low light conditions. The telephoto   lens produces the least amount of distortion and  is therefore best suited for portraits. However,   it has the smallest aperture and therefore  achieves the worst results in low light. If you now tap and hold one of the numbers, a kind  of zoom slider appears and you can swipe left and   right to zoom in and out more precisely.  For this tutorial I use an iPhone 17 Pro,   so you can see 0.5, 1,2, 4, 7 and 49 as numbers.  The iPhone 17 and 17 Air have only a 10x digital   zoom range.These numbers of course stand for  the different cameras of your iPhone. If the   quality of your photos is important to you,  you should avoid a zoom factor between the   numbers. And you should also avoid a zoom  factor above 8, because in all these cases   it results in a digital crop of the image. You can also zoom in and out by using two   fingers. In this case you can always  read the current zoom factor here. When you capture a picture with your Iphone,  you will of course determine the framing on   the display. So what should be on the picture,  how should the picture composition look like.   Basically the framing of your picture  will correspond to this outer frame. In   many cases you can also see the area outside  this frame.This exceeding part is slightly   darkened. It is supposed to help you to find  the right framing, but it is not captured. For the right composition the grid also helps  you. So you can stick to the Rule of Thirds,   for example, which is important for a good  composition. And this white line is supposed   to help you keep your iPhone straight when  taking a photo and avoid a crooked horizon.   It appears when you rotate your iPhone. As soon  as the line turns yellow, you can be sure that   the horizon is straight. If the grid or  the level do not appear on your iPhone,   you can activate them in the settings  of the camera app under Composition. In addition to the right framing, focus  and exposure are crucial for a good photo.   The iPhone's camera sets focus and exposure  automatically and does an excellent job. Faces   are recognized perfectly. You can see this by the  yellow frame that appears when the camera detects   a face. And actually, with the iphone you can also  get a blurred background or foreground naturally,   for example when you capture objects in the near  foreground and the background is far away. So   it is quite possible that certain areas of the  image are in focus and others are not. Therefore   it can sometimes be necessary to adjust the  focus manually. You do this by tapping on   the object or person in the image that should  be in focus. Now your iPhone also adjusts the   exposure to the new focus. You can also correct  the exposure manually. You do this by tapping on   the sun next to the focus square and by swiping  up or down. Upwards the image becomes brighter,   downwards it becomes darker. You can also lock  the focus and the exposure. Suppose you set the   focus and/or the exposure manually and want them  to stay locked even when you move the phone. You   can do this by tapping and holding on the  appropriate area. Now the AE/AF Lock sign   appears in yellow at the top. You can now move  your phone without changing focus and exposure.   This can be useful for example if you want to  change the framing after setting the focus. The ultra-wide camera of the iPhone 17 & 17 Pro  has a very special feature. It can focus extremely   close. Apple calls this Macro Mode, because by  getting very close, you can capture small subjects   greatly magnified. You don't need to have the  ultra-wide camera activated for this. The iPhone   automatically switches to Macro Mode when the  distance to an object becomes less than 14 cm.   So if the ultra-wide camera is not active, the  iPhone automatically switches from the main camera   or the telephoto lens to the ultra-wide camera.  You will notice this when there are strange   image jumps. This happens automatically. If you  want to have more control over the Macro Mode,   you can activate the Macro Control feature in  the settings of the camera app. Now, whenever   the Macro Mode is active, a yellow flower appears  at the bottom left. By tapping on the flower,   you can now also deactivate the Macro Mode. Personally, I think that the macro mode is   a great feature. You should try  it out - the results are amazing. Apart from these basic features there are  a few additional settings and features. To   use them you have to open an additional  menu. You can do this by tapping on the   icon with the 6 small dots in the top right  corner or by swiping up with your finger to   the left or right of the shutter button. An  additional menu will now appear where you can   activate or deactivate certain features.  On the top left you will find the flash.   You can set it to automatic, on or off. Notice how  the flash symbol at the top of the screen changes. The icon for the night mode only appears  at the bottom when it is dark. The Night   Mode will dramatically change the results in  low light conditions. Normally the Night Mode   will activate automatically when it is very dark.  When the night mode icon at the top turns yellow,   you know that the night mode is active. In  NightMode your IPhone uses longer exposure times.   Depending on how dark it is, your iPhone will  need a shorter or longer exposure time to get   a good result. You can see the duration of the  automatically set exposure time right next to   the icon, for example 1 or 2 seconds. During the  exposure you should hold your iPhone as still as   possible. At the top of the preview image a timer  appears which counts down the remaining time.   If you shake or move the iPhone, the device will  recognize this and two crosshairs will appear,   one yellow and one white. You should move the  iPhone in a way that the yellow crosshair lies   directly on top of the white one. This helps to  reduce movements and you can get a better result.  By tapping the Night Mode icon, you can disable  the Night Mode or activate Night Mode Max. Night   Mode Max can be extremely useful. With Night Mode  Max, the exposure time is extended even further.   This doesn't make the image brighter, but if you  have set up your iPhone so that it is stable and   thus eliminate camera shake, you can achieve the  best possible results in low light conditions   with a longer exposure time and Night Mode Max. And since the best option for setting up your   iPhone so that it is stable is always the one  you have with you, I looked for a high-quality   case with an integrated stand for my iPhone 17  Pro. And I'm very happy to have discovered the   new MagicStand case from Casekoo. Casekoo has  also agreed to sponsor this video. The Magic   Stand is the first stand that can be rotated a  full 360 degrees and is permanently integrated   into the case. This allows you to position the  iPhone vertically or horizontally. In many cases,   this means you no longer need a tripod to film  yourself. You can also use the Magic Stand to   read a book, watch a movie, or—as I often do  myself—use your iPhone for video conferences.   This case from Casekoo, das es natürlich auch für  das iPhone 16 oder 15 gibt, is available in all   kinds of colors, of course. While I often  prefer simple colors like this deep blue,   I have to admit, the cosmic orange  color looks very cool and would of   course go really well with the orange iPhone  17 Pro. And the case has another advantage:   the camera cutout has a relatively high edge,  which means the lenses are very well protected.   You can also use the stand to hold your iPhone  securely so that you don't drop it when taking   selfies. If you like the case, use the link  in the description for an additional discount. The icon at the top center refers to Live Photos,  one of my favorite iphone photo features. Here,   too, the automatic is normally active. You can  also turn LivePhotos on or off permanently here.  With LivePhotos your iphone captures a clip  of one and a half seconds before and after   the photo was taken. Therefore a short video clip  of three seconds is created. You can watch this   miniclip by tapping and holding on the screen  when viewing your photo. You can change the   so-called Key Photo within your clip and you  can also add cool effects to your Live Photo.   It's very easy to do. To add effects, you open  your Live Photo and tap on the Live icon in the   upper left corner. You can add a loop to repeat  your shot continuously or bounce to repeat your   shot alternately forwards and backwards. I  find Long Exposure especially interesting.   This simulates a long exposure. It works well  with moving lights or for example with water.   If you tap on the settings icon at the bottom and  then on the Live icon you can also change the key   photo and shorten the duration of the clip. It  can be extremely useful to change the key photo,   for example if your subject has closed his or  her eyes in the previous key photo. We will take   a look at the other editing features later. You can tell that your iphone is capturing   a Live Photo by the yellow Live  icon at the top of the screen.  The next icon is a timer. This allows you to set  that the image is not taken until three , five or   ten seconds after pressing the shutter button.  This can be useful if you want to take a group   selfie and place your iPhone on a tripod or in low  light with longer exposures to avoid any shaking. On the left, you will find a very important  feature, the Exposure Slider. With this feature   you can make your image brighter or darker, that  means you can overexpose or underexpose your image   a little bit in relation to the settings of the  automatic exposure control and this for a maximum   of 2 stops. But wait, wasn't there already the sun  icon for that? Yes, but with the exposure slider   you can make smaller and more precise adjustments  and you have a little more control. In addition,   the exposure correction will be kept and will  stay the same if you take more photos. I have   to say that the automatic of the iPhone protects  so-called highlights very well and there are   hardly any overexposed areas in the pictures.  So for such cases you hardly need any exposure   correction. It's more that you might want to  capture a darker or brighter image to create   a certain mood. For example, many people believe  that the iPhone exposes the images too brightly   and prefer a slightly negative value here. But  beware, if you no longer need this function,   you should deactivate it again. Otherwise, all  your pictures will be under- or overexposed. This icon stands for the photographic styles. The  Photographic Styles are not to be confused with   filters. In its default setting, the iPhone  produces a relatively natural look that is   relatively close to reality. Other smartphone  manufacturers, for example, prefer a more vivid   look with more saturation or more contrast. With  the Photographic Styles you can now change the   general look of your iPhone photos. Unlike  a filter, it does not simply apply a color   to the whole image. Photographic Styles adjust  specific colors in certain parts of your photos   to create the exact look you want. And unlike  with older iPhone models, the iPhone 17 allows   you to change and customise the photographic  style even after the photo has been taken. There   are basically two types of styles: undertones,  which mainly influence the undertone of the skin,   and mood styles, which give the whole image  a special look. Undertones include Cool Rose,   Neutral, Bright, Rose Gold, gold or Amber, while  under Mood you will find for example Vibrant or   Dramatic, Cosy or Stark Black & White for a very  high-contrast black and white photo. You can use   the slider at the bottom to adjust the intensity  of the effect, whereby the effect is neutralised,   at least as far as the colour is concerned, if  you drag the slider all the way to the left.   You can then make further adjustments using  the field in the centre. To put it simply,   the further you drag the slider to the right, the  more saturated the effect becomes; if you drag   it upwards, parts of the image are brightened and  the contrast is reduced; if you drag it downwards,   the image appears darker and the contrast  is increased. One thing should be clear:   The style remains active even when you close the  app. So if some of your photos look a bit strange,   this could be due to an unintentionally active  photographic style. And Apple has come up with   a guide to help you find your ideal style and  set it as the new standard. When you open the   settings in the Camera app, under Photographic  Styles you can select 4 photos , ideally photos   that have different subjects, and can now  see at a glance what effects the respective   photographic style has on your photos. You can  then select your favourite style, adjust it if   necessary and save it as the new default. This  allows you to define your own personal style for   your iPhone photos. However, only the undertones  are available here in the settings. And as I said,   if you don't like a photo with a certain style,  you can now, in contrast to older iPhones,   open the photo and adjust the photographic  style later via the settings and styles. With the icon to the right of the Photographic  Styles you can change the aspect ratio of your   photos. A normal photo sensor delivers a  photo in a 4:3 format. You can also set   a square aspect ratio or a 16:9 aspect ratio  which is more typical for video recordings. You   should not forget that you can also change  the aspect ratio when editing your photos. Before we take a look at the portrait mode, let's  take a closer look at the new selfie camera,   the so-called Center Stage Camera of the iPhone  17. The selfie camera also supports features such   as the Night Mode and the Live Photo Mode. And you  can zoom in and out using the two small arrows,   whereby this zoom effect is significantly bigger  than on older iPhones. But what's really cool is   that you can use this icon here to switch from  portrait to landscape format without turning the   iPhone. Why would that be useful? For example,  if you want to take a group selfie, you no   longer have to hold your iPhone sideways, which  was not only awkward but also rarely produced   good results. This new feature is possible because  the selfie camera's sensor is now square. And the   CenterStage feature, which you can activate via  the icon at the top, can also set the optimal zoom   factor or format automatically when you activate  Autozoom or Autorotate here. The camera will now   automatically zoom out or rotate the image if  not everyone of the group fits in the frame.  It is also interesting that the Selfie-camera  shows you a mirrored image in the preview,   but captures the image without mirroring it. The  picture then looks different from the preview.   If this bothers you, you can change this  in the settings. When capturing selfies,   remember that you can use the volume buttons  as shutter. This is especially useful here. If we now switch to portrait mode, we find  the usual settings for flash, timer, exposure,   styles and aspect ratio in the menu. LivePhoto is  not available. However, there are two new settings   here, different portrait lighting effects, which  you can select here by swiping to the left and   right and the F-Stop or Aperture. As explained  in the beginning, in portrait mode your iPhone   creates a blurred background, as if you had taken  the picture with a Mirrorless or DSLR and a fast   lens. In general, the smaller the number of  the F-stop the stronger the effect will be,   that means the blurrier the background will  be. The bigger the number the smaller the   effect will be. Just like it would be on a  bigger camera. Basically the feature works   incredibly well. Only in very small details,  such as protruding hairs, can you sometimes   spot imperfections. As already mentioned the  portrait mode also works with objects. But   especially with objects you have to make sure  that there is an appropriate distance between   camera and object to activate the feature. The  camera tells you this at the top of the screen. And Portrait Lighting is also a great feature.  It is designed to simulate professional lighting   techniques. If you've been into photography,  you'll know that good lighting often makes   a bigger difference than the actual camera.  You can select different lighting effects by   swiping to the left and right. You can also  see the effect in the preview. With Studio   Light the face is well exposed by a soft  light. It reminds you of a beauty filter,   with Contour Light the external areas of the  face or the object are darkened a bit. This   gives the picture a little more depth and drama.  With Stage Light the subject is isolated in the   spotlight. The outer area is completely darkened.  Stage Light Mono creates the same effect in black   and white and High-Key Light Mono does not darken  but lightens the outer area, for example like a   portrait against a white background. If you find  the effect exaggerated or too weak, you can adjust   the intensity. In the menu tap on the Intensity  icon and now you can adjust the effect by dragging   the slider to the left or right. Also note that  you can change and adjust the Portrait Lighting   and F-Stop when editing your portrait. And not  only that - when you edit your portrait, you can   even move the focus point using the yellow frame. Here on the left, you can also switch between   the main camera, the 2x Crop and the telephoto  lens on the 17 Pro and Pro Max. For best results   of close-ups of faces, I recommend using  the telephoto lens. And there's something   else you should know. If you are in the normal  photo mode and your iPhone recognizes a face,   an F icon appears at the bottom left. This means  you can activate the portrait feature in normal   photo mode with a simple tap and don't have  to switch to portrait mode. And you can even   activate the portrait feature when editing the  photo - it's only important that your iPhone has   detected a face when taking the picture. By  the way, this also works with dogs and cats. With the Panorama mode you can capture an  extremely wide field of view. As the name   suggests, this is great for capturing  landscapes or cityscapes. Basically,   several images are combined. To do this, start  recording and move your camera along the arrow   from left to right. If you tap on the arrow you  can also change the direction and capture the   panorama from right to left. Make sure that  the arrow is moving along the yellow line   during the recording. If you do not succeed,  you will see unsightly waves in the image.   Apart from that there are hardly any settings in  this mode. You can change the lens and that's it. Now that we've learnt about most of the features  of the camera app, it's time to take a closer look   at the camera control button. And the button has  some very interesting features. As mentioned at   the beginning, you can use it to open the camera  app. When the camera app is open, you can take   a photo or start and stop a video with a single  press. If you press and hold the button in photo   mode, you can record a QuickCapture video, similar  to the Capture button of the app. But that's not   all. The brilliant thing is that if you swipe  with light pressure up or down on the button or   to the left or right, depending on how you hold  the iPhone, you can change various settings.   As you can see, I change the exposure by swiping  up and down, just like with the Exposure Control   feature. You can also change other settings  with the camera control button. To do this,   lightly press the button twice and a menu will  appear. You can now select the feature you want to   adjust by swiping. In addition to the exposure,  you can change the F-Stop for portrait shots,   the zoom factor, whereby the zoom for all values  between the various cameras leads to a digital   crop and a reduction in image quality. This is  why it is also possible to switch only between   the cameras, which is clearly the better option.  At the bottom you can change the styles and even   adjust the tone of your style. when you change  settings with the camera control button, all other   information disappears from the screen - so you  can better assess the effects on your photo. Apple   calls this Clean Preview - you can deactivate  this feature in the settings of the camera app.   And you can deactivate single functions and  customize the functionality of the button. And the button has another very useful  feature. When you press and hold it,   you activate visual intelligence. Your iPhone  can now recognize plants or animals. You can   find information about a business, such as the  opening hours of a restaurant. You can interact   with text—summarize it or have it translated. Or  you can ask ChatGPT for an analysis of the image,   or you can search Google for similar  images immediately and directly. However,   the function must be activated under Camera  Control and Launch Visual Intelligence. Now let's have a quick look at the editing tools.  If you tap on the small image of your last shot   in the lower left corner, you will see your  last image in full size and can edit it in many   different ways. Today I will only give you a brief  overview and point out a few important things. If   you swipe through your last photos, you can see in  the upper left corner if your photo is a portrait   photo or a live photo. If not, this area remains  blank. This is important because portrait photos   and live photos allow different editing steps.  Generally, the editing menu opens by pressing the   settings button here below. As already explained,  here you can subsequently change or customise the   style used. With the adjust icon you can make  basic adjustments to the photo. You can adjust   the colors, exposure, white balance, saturation,  sharpness and so on. And by clicking the   icon on the right you can rotate, crop or adjust  the aspect ratio of the photo. With Clean up,   you can use AI to remove unwanted objects from an  image. And via the pen icon, you can add markups,   stickers, text, and so on. If your shot is a  portrait, as mentioned before, you can use the   editing tools to change the portrait lighting  and the depth of field, that is, the F-stop. It's time to take a look at a few more important  options in the settings of the camera app. With   preserve settings you can for example set the  camera to remember certain settings. For example,   you always want to use the same photographic  style, the same exposure correction or you always   want to start the app in video mode. Then you can  enable here that the app remembers your settings. The camera of your iPhone can automatically  detect text and you can then copy and edit   this text. You don't even have to take a photo  for that. When your iPhone detects a text, you   can see this by the yellow borders and the small  text icon. If you tap on the text icon, you can   select parts of the detected text, copy it, use  it for a search, translate it or even share it.   This feature can be very useful. If you don't need  it, you can deactivate it here in the settings. Now a few very important settings. Under Formats  you can choose between High Efficiency and Most   Compatible. If High Efficiency is activated,  your iPhone saves the photos in the HEIF format.   This saves a lot of storage space and the  quality is said to be even better than that   of a JPEG file. However, Windows, for example,  cannot automatically handle this format. To use   your photos on Windows without an extension, you  must convert them to JPEG. If you activate “most   compatible” here, the photos will already  be saved in the JPEG format on your iPhone.  The cameras of your iPhone have sensors with 48  megapixels. Nevertheless, to save storage space,   in the default settings the photos are  saved in a resolution of 12 megapixels.   If you often want to view your photos on a  larger screen, you can set a higher default   resolution of 24 megapixels here.  I would definitely recommend this. And you can also save your photos in the full  resolution of 48 megapixels. To do this, activate   ProRAW & Resolution Control. HEIC is used as the  standard format here as well. But be careful -   according to Apple itself, the 48 megapixel  photos have more resolution, but they also   lose dynamic range. So for a better dynamic range  you should better take photos with 24 megapixels. If, on the other hand, you want to edit  your photo heavily and therefore want to   save as much information as  possible in the photo file,   then you should activate Apple Pro Raw. The  photos require considerably more storage space,   but contain more information for editing. To ensure that your iPhone actually takes   a photo with 48 megapixels or in the RAW  format, you must activate this option in   the camera app before taking the photo  by selecting it in the upper left corner. And with this I want to say goodbye for today.  If the video was interesting or helpful for you,   give me a Like as feedback. If you want to  support this channel you can use the link in   the video description to buy me a coffee. If you want to learn even more about how   to get the best results with your iPhone camera,   check out my tutorials on iPhone photography  or editing photos on the iPhone. You can also   find a list of my best iPhone Camera  Tutorials in the video description. There will be more videos about the IPhone 17  and 17 Pro. So stay tuned and see you next time!
